revisions_applied: REPLACED: '68% retail cashless' → BNM AR 2025: 18.4 billion e-payment transactions, 538 per capita
REPLACED: '42% in 2020' → BNM AR 2020: 170 e-payments per capita; 42% was % of GDP not retail
REPLACED: 'DuitNow QR 1.2 billion / RM85 billion' → BNM AR 2025: 3 BILLION transactions, ~3M touchpoints; BNM AR 2024: RM31.1B value
CORRECTED: '28 million users' = TNG single operator (BusinessToday Dec 2024), not all-Malaysia
CORRECTED: '35% rural vs 78% urban' → PayNet 2022: 36.2% urban / 63.5% non-urban STILL using cash daily
CORRECTED: 'RM2.8B e-wallet fraud up 85%' → RM2.8B is total scam impact, not e-wallet specific. BNM also reported RM1.2B fraud losses AVERTED in 2025
REVERSED: 'unauthorised transactions and social engineering fastest growing' → BNM Financial Stability Review 1H 2025: unauthorised CURBED, authorised social engineering RISING
ADDED: NSRC 2025 — 146,167 calls, 138,915 frozen accounts, RM34.53M recovered, now 24/7 under police leadership
ADDED: BNM 2025 Basic Banking Services policy mandates cash withdrawal/deposit access
ADDED: International parallels — India FRI, Singapore SRF & Money Lock, Indonesia 0% MDR, Kenya M-Pesa duty of care

cards.text: Malaysia made 18.4 billion e-payment transactions in 2025 — about 538 per Malaysian. The headline growth is real. The framing of the divide is not.
cards.sub: BNM Annual Report 2025 confirms total e-payment transactions grew 25% to 18.4 billion in 2025, with 538 transactions per capita and RM831 billion in retail e-payment value. DuitNow QR transaction volume doubled to 3 billion in 2025 with nearly 3 million registered touchpoints by year-end. Cash use, however, is far more persistent than headline cashless figures suggest. PayNet's Digital Payments Insights Study found 48.4% of daily expenses were still being paid in cash, with 36.2% of urban residents and 63.5% of non-urban residents still using cash for daily expenses. Adoption is climbing fast, but two Malaysias are still moving at very different speeds.

cards.h: DuitNow QR doubled to 3 billion transactions in 2025, with nearly 3 million touchpoints. The infrastructure is expanding faster than the public conversation has kept up with.
cards.s: Bank Negara Malaysia's 2025 Annual Report confirms DuitNow QR transaction volume doubled to 3 billion in 2025, with almost 3 million registered DuitNow QR touchpoints by end-2025. The 2024 Annual Report had reported end-2024 acceptance points at 2.6 million and 2024 transaction value at RM31.1 billion. Average e-money transaction size has climbed to RM43 in 2025 from RM33 in 2024 — meaning Malaysians are not just transacting more often, they are using digital channels for higher-value transactions. The Retail Digitalisation Initiative (ReDI) has helped onboard tens of thousands of small traders into digital payment acceptance, though East Malaysia onboarding still lags Peninsular targets. Touch 'n Go remains the dominant operator with around 28 million registered users and over 24 million verified users in 2025 — but registered users at one platform is not the same as a national active e-wallet user count.
cards.d: Sources: BNM Annual Report 2025 (as quoted by Bernama 31 Mar 2026); BNM Annual Report 2024 (as quoted by Bernama 24 Mar 2025); ReDI initiative reporting (Bernama, Armizan Mohd Ali); Touch 'n Go Digital announcements (BusinessToday Dec 2024). Note: '1.2 billion / RM85 billion' DuitNow QR figures removed — verified 2025 volume is 3 billion.

cards.h: Cash is more persistent than headline numbers suggest. PayNet found 36.2% of urban Malaysians and 63.5% of non-urban Malaysians still use cash for daily expenses.
cards.s: PayNet's Digital Payments Insights Study reported 48.4% of daily expenses were still being paid in cash in 2022, with 36.2% of urban respondents and 63.5% of non-urban respondents still using cash for daily expenses. Bank Negara's Financial Inclusion Framework 2023-2026 explicitly notes that reliance on cash and traditional banking persists particularly among low-income, elderly, and rural communities, owing to a mix of digital literacy gaps and connectivity constraints. BNM's 2025 Basic Banking Services policy goes further: it requires covered financial service providers to maintain cash withdrawal and cash or coin deposit services for basic banking accounts — an explicit policy choice to keep cash access alive while digital adoption grows. Inside that picture, PayNet's regional findings are not a simple 'East Malaysia lags' story: the survey found businesses in Northern, Central, and East Malaysia were moving toward digital payments as the preferred mode, while the East Coast and Southern regions were the ones still leaning more on cash. State-level digital wallets like Sarawak's S Pay Global have emerged as locally accountable alternatives that complement national rails.
cards.d: Sources: PayNet Digital Payments Insights Study (2022); BNM Financial Inclusion Framework 2023-2026; BNM Basic Banking Services Policy Document (2025); S Pay Global / Sarawak Pay public materials. Note: '35% rural vs 78% urban' figure removed — conflated cash-use with cashless-adoption and used 2022 PayNet data with wrong framing.

cards.h: BNM reported RM2.8 billion in scam impact in 2025 — but most of that is socially-engineered fraud where victims authorise the transfer themselves. RM1.2 billion was averted before it left.
cards.s: BNM Annual Report 2025 reported approximately RM2.8 billion in scam impact across Malaysia in 2025 — a figure that covers all online and financial scams, not just e-wallet fraud. Crucially, BNM also reported that around RM1.2 billion in fraud losses were averted in 2025 — many times the actual losses reported directly to BNM. The Financial Stability Review for the first half of 2025 explicitly stated that unauthorised scam cases were curbed, while authorised scams involving social engineering rose. This is the opposite of an unauthorised-transaction story: most losses now come from victims being psychologically manipulated into approving the transfer themselves. The National Scam Response Centre (NSRC), which moved to 24/7 operation under direct police leadership, handled 146,167 complaint calls in 2025, implemented freezing actions on 138,915 accounts, and recovered around RM34.53 million for victims. The headline 'fraud up' narrative is simpler than the underlying picture; the harder truth is that the system is increasingly defending against persuasion, not penetration.
cards.d: Sources: BNM Annual Report 2025; BNM Financial Stability Review 1H 2025; NSRC reporting (The Star, 3 Mar 2026); Bernama reporting on Home Ministry online fraud figures. Note: 'RM2.8 billion in e-wallet fraud up 85%' framing removed — this is total scam impact, not e-wallet specific. 'Unauthorised transactions fastest growing' reversed — BNM says unauthorised CURBED while authorised social engineering rising.

cards.h: The real cashless question is not 'how fast can we get to 100%' — it is who gets left without working alternatives when the cash window closes.
cards.text: The numbers Malaysia loves to publish are growth numbers: 18.4 billion e-payment transactions, 3 billion DuitNow QR transactions, RM831 billion in retail e-payment value. The numbers Malaysia publishes less often are persistence numbers: 63.5% of non-urban Malaysians still using cash for daily expenses, BNM's own Financial Inclusion Framework warning that low-income, elderly, and rural communities remain cash-reliant, and the Basic Banking Services policy quietly mandating that branches maintain cash deposit and withdrawal services. These are not contradictions. They are evidence that Malaysia is running two payment systems in parallel — one for digitally connected and middle-class users in urban centres, and one for everyone else. The political instinct is to hide the second system to keep the cashless story clean. The honest path is to invest in both, and to be specific about the consumer protection gaps that are getting worse on the digital side: authorised social-engineering scams rising, NSRC stretched across 146,000+ complaints, and a public conversation about fraud that still treats it as a technical problem when most losses now come from persuasion. Singapore is testing 'Money Lock' to let users freeze portions of their funds against scams. India deploys real-time fraud risk indicators on mobile numbers. Indonesia waives merchant fees for the smallest hawkers. Malaysia has the rails. The protection layer is the part that has not been built out.

Malaysia's e-payment growth is genuine. 18.4 billion transactions in 2025, an average of 538 per Malaysian, RM831 billion in retail e-payment value, DuitNow QR doubling to 3 billion transactions, and average e-money transaction sizes climbing from RM33 to RM43. These are real achievements. But cash is more persistent than the success narrative implies — 63.5% of non-urban Malaysians and 36.2% of urban Malaysians still use cash for daily expenses, and BNM's own policy framework has correctly chosen to keep cash access mandatory rather than phase it out. The consumer protection picture is also more complicated than headlines suggest. The RM2.8 billion in 2025 scam impact is not e-wallet fraud — it is total scam losses across channels, dominated by authorised transfers where victims were socially engineered, not by unauthorised account takeovers. BNM averted around RM1.2 billion before it left. The NSRC handled 146,167 complaints, froze 138,915 accounts, and recovered around RM34.53 million for victims. The honest path forward is not faster cashless adoption alone. It is honest measurement of who is being left behind, rules that protect cash access at essential services, dedicated supervision of the social-engineering scam threat, and consumer-protection tools (shared liability frameworks, fund-locking features, real-time risk scoring) that other jurisdictions have already started to deploy. Malaysia has the rails. The next decade is about whether the protection layer catches up before the next wave of vulnerable users gets pushed onto a system that cannot reliably protect them.

sources: BNM Annual Report 2025 (18.4B transactions, 538 per capita, RM831B retail, 3B DuitNow QR, RM43 avg); BNM Annual Report 2024 (RM31.1B DuitNow QR value, 2.6M acceptance points); BNM Financial Inclusion Framework 2023-2026; BNM Basic Banking Services Policy 2025; BNM Financial Stability Review 1H 2025; PayNet Digital Payments Insights Study 2022; NSRC reporting (The Star, 3 Mar 2026); Bernama coverage of BNM AR (Mar 2025 and 2026); Touch 'n Go Digital announcements; ReDI initiative reporting; S Pay Global / Sarawak Pay materials
